assets.name: lecture_08_figure_02.png
assets.rationale: This frame gives the clearest combined view of the board layout at this moment: the wave-like sketches on the left are fully visible, and the repeated second-derivative equations on the right are more legible than in the neighboring frames. It is also the least affected by gesture blur, so it is the most useful single reference for later note writing and cautious reconstruction.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: linear, not too many derivatives in the equations,

assets.name: lecture_08_figure_03.png
assets.rationale: This frame gives the most complete and legible view of the board at this moment. It preserves the top quadratic-form expression more fully than the later crops and also keeps the full 2x2 matrix visible, making it the best single reference for note writing about the matrix being interpreted as a metric.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: in fact the matrix is called a metric

assets.name: lecture_08_figure_04.png
assets.rationale: This is the best of the four because the line-element expression at the top is the least obstructed by hand motion, and the left-side geometric sketch of the curved surface is still clearly visible. The lecturer still blocks part of the middle, but this frame preserves the most usable combination of equation and diagram for later reconstruction.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: what the metric tensor is and the metric

assets.name: lecture_08_figure_05.png
assets.rationale: This frame gives the clearest overall board evidence for the moment: the tensor-style line-element expression on the left is more readable than in the pointing frames because Susskind's arm no longer covers it, while the earlier quadratic-form matrix on the right remains visible as context. That makes it the most useful single reference for writing notes about the metric tensor and its point-by-point variation.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: the metric tensor vary from point to point
assets.caption_hint: metric tensor in the line element
